interviewStages: Behavioral: design empathy, craft, written communication
codingTopics: Frontend data structures: DOM trees and scene graphs
String and array manipulation for layout and parsing
Graph traversal for component and layer trees
systemDesignTopics: Design the rendering engine for a visual web-design canvas with nested layers
Design an undo/redo and history system for a collaborative design editor
Design a system that exports a visual design into production React code
Design real-time multiplayer editing of a shared design document

coding: Given a nested layer tree on a canvas, hit-test which layer sits under a click point. Talk through the data structure and complexity.
Implement an undo/redo stack for canvas edits with grouped and coalesced operations. Discuss your approach and edge cases.
Interpolate a spring or easing animation between two layout states over time. Talk through the math and edge cases.
Given absolute-positioned elements, compute the overall bounding box and detect overlaps. Discuss complexity.
Serialize and deserialize a component tree so a design can be saved and restored exactly. Talk through the trade-offs.
behavioral: Tell me about a time you obsessed over the craft and polish of a UI or interaction.
Describe a time you made something feel measurably faster or smoother for users.
Tell me about collaborating closely with designers on a hard frontend problem.
Describe a time you dug into browser internals or rendering to fix a tough bug.
systemDesign: Design the rendering engine for a nested-layer design canvas. Cover the scene graph, invalidation, and performance.
Design undo/redo and history for a collaborative editor. Cover operation modeling, coalescing, and conflicts.
Design real-time multiplayer editing of a design document. Cover presence, conflict resolution, and sync.
values: Framer's mission is to make the web more creative. What does great craft in software mean to you?
Tell me about a time you cared more about the end user's creative experience than shipping fast.
How do you balance visual and interaction polish against engineering constraints?
other: How does Framer compare to Webflow or Squarespace?

Updated May 5, 2026 · techinterview.org

⏱ 1 min read

Framer
framer.com ↗

Framer is the design-and-publish platform for modern websites — competing with Webflow on the visual builder space, with stronger design-tool ergonomics. Amsterdam-headquartered, fast-growing. The interview emphasizes design tool engineering, browser internals, and animation work.

Process

Recruiter screen → 60-minute coding pair → onsite virtual: 2 coding, 1 system design, 1 craft deep-dive, 1 behavioral. Cycle: 3–5 weeks.

What they actually ask

- Design a real-time collaborative canvas (similar to Figma)

- Design CSS code generation from visual primitives

- Design Framer Motion-style animation engine

- Coding: practical TypeScript, often with browser internals or animation framing

- Behavioral: design empathy, craft, written communication

Levels and comp (2026)

- SE II: EUR 75K–95K total (Amsterdam)

- Senior SE: EUR 105K–135K

- Staff: EUR 145K–185K

- Principal: EUR 200K+

European-tier comp; below US peers. Strong work-life balance.

Prep priorities

- Be fluent in TypeScript/React (the bulk of the codebase)

- Understand browser internals: DOM, CSS, layout, paint

- Brush up on web animation (Framer Motion, CSS animations)

Frequently Asked Questions

Is Framer remote-friendly?

Hybrid in Amsterdam (HQ), San Francisco. Some engineering roles fully remote within EU.

How does Framer compare to Webflow or Squarespace?

Framer wins on design ergonomics for designer-power-users. Webflow is more developer-friendly. Squarespace is design-templated. Framer pays European tier.

What is the engineering culture?

Design-focused, technically rigorous, calm pace. Strong fit for engineers who care about craft and aesthetics.
